storm.summary_layperson: Thunderstorms are expected from about 11 AM through 2 PM today — a 90% chance per the National Weather Service — and the Long Island Sound's typical storm-dampening effect is weak today, so the cove should expect similar conditions to surrounding inland areas.
storm.local_effect_blurb: With only a 4°F water–air temperature gap, the marine layer is too weak to deflect incoming storms, and strong upper-level shear (35.7 kt) gives any organized cell the structure to punch through to the coastline.
storm.detail_html:
The regional storm setup is active and serious. The Storm Prediction Center has expanded an Enhanced Risk for severe thunderstorms to include much of the Lower Hudson Valley, NE NJ, and the NYC metro — a broad area that covers Greenwich. The OKX Area Forecast Discussion cites a mean surface-based CAPE near 1,000 J/kg for the NYC metro this afternoon, with ensemble maximums reaching 2,000–3,000 J/kg just southwest of us. The NWS Upton forecast for this area shows a 90% chance of showers and thunderstorms from 11 AM through 2 PM — falling directly inside the 10:00–15:00 wind window. The main severe hazard per the AFD is damaging wind gusts.

The local protective mechanism — the Long Island Sound's cool water creating a stable marine layer that weakens incoming storms — is weaker than normal today. The air–water temperature gap is only 4°F (water near 70°F, air 73°F), well below the 10°F threshold needed to activate meaningful coastal storm suppression. The Sound effect is rated moderate at most today, not the strong protective effect seen on hotter days with cooler water.

The more concerning local factor is upper-level wind shear at 35.7 kt (westerly). Strong shear organizes storm updrafts into long-lived, structured cells. On days with even a borderline sea breeze present — and a weak one is active today — convective cells arriving at the sea-breeze convergence boundary can intensify rather than weaken. This punch-through scenario is why the local effect is rated amplifying: the cove is unlikely to escape the storm any better than inland areas, and could briefly experience intensified outflow at the convergence zone.

There is a notable disagreement between the deterministic HRRR model and the NWS/AFD consensus on timing. HRRR keeps CAPE near zero through 1 PM before building to 380–1,150 J/kg in the extended afternoon period. The NWS forecast calls for storms beginning at 11 AM. The AFD attributes the timing uncertainty to wildfire smoke suppressing surface heating this morning. The NWS mesoscale ensemble (HREF) is the basis for the 11 AM call and is the more reliable tool for this type of smoke-complicated setup — weight NWS timing over the HRRR point forecast on today's timing question.

What would change the call upward: any watch or warning issued by NWS Upton, or radar showing organized cells ≥45 dBZ within 25 miles approaching from the WSW–WNW (the dominant shear vector). What would push the risk lower: smoke remaining thick through noon, CAPE failing to build above 500 J/kg, and storms tracking south of the area along the I-95 corridor.

wind.regime_label: Light gradient wind from the SSW — no sea-breeze thermal driver in play

hourly.shift_note: thunderstorm window opens — gradient holding for now
storm window active; convective outflow gusts possible
wind stall and W shift — convective outflow or pre-storm calm

wind.tactics_blurb: The early window (10–11 AM) has clean, light SSW flow straight up the cove — 6–8 knots, center-course start, no tactical pressure. Thunderstorm risk from 11 AM onward makes sustained racing impractical; the Race Committee should be ready to hoist AP by 10:45. If racing resumes after 1 PM in a post-storm W gradient, work the right side.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
This is a light gradient wind day — not a sea-breeze day. The inland air–water temperature gap is only 3.8°F (Westchester Airport 73°F vs. Long Island Sound water 70°F), below the 5°F threshold needed to drive a thermal sea breeze. The SSW flow at 6–8 knots in the early window is a modest synoptic gradient, not thermally driven. There is no sunset-collapse story — the window ends more than five hours before sunset.

The SSW direction in the 10:00–12:00 window aligns cleanly with the cove's N–S axis. Flow from 200–210° enters the cove mouth with minimal obstruction from Greenwich Point (Tod's Point), making pressure reasonably even across the starting area. Current RYC readings (SSW 211°, 4.1 kt, 3.2 kt 10-minute average) run lighter than the over-water buoy (SW 9.6 kt) and the HRRR model forecast (~9 kt at the grid point). The cove is sheltered enough that 6–8 knots at the line is the right expectation. There is a small model/obs direction divergence — HRRR shows 208° while the western LIS station cluster is already at 223° (SW). On this gradient-wind day, the sea-breeze maturation correction does not apply, but RYC's 211° SSW is the right cove baseline over the raw model direction.

The shift character in the early window will be slow and persistent rather than oscillating. A light gradient day without a mature sea breeze does not produce the rhythmic 10–15 minute oscillations that a well-developed thermal day offers. Expect slow direction drifts with gust-lull cycling. Don't tack on headers expecting them to come back — commit to the lifted tack.

The dominant tactical question for this window is not which side of the course to favor — it is whether to race at all between 11 AM and 1 PM. The NWS forecast calls for a 90% chance of thunderstorms from 11 AM through 2 PM, with gusts to 20 knots and the AFD warning of damaging wind gusts from organized severe cells. The HRRR model shows the wind stalling to 4 knots and shifting W around 1 PM before a stronger W gradient rebuilds at 10–14 knots post-storm. The Race Committee should have AP and lightning protocols ready from 10:45 AM onward.

For any racing that resumes after 1 PM in the post-storm W gradient: cove geometry for W–WNW flow tends to be steady in direction but often produces holes near the CT shore (the cove opens south, not west, so W flow crosses the cove axis rather than funneling up it). A center-to-right start strategy gives access to the stronger mid-Sound pressure. Conditions post-storm can be directionally unstable for the first 30–60 minutes as outflow boundaries decay — allow time before resuming.

data_caveats: RAP CAPE unavailable; instability from OKX AFD mentions only (1,000 J/kg mean SBCAPE cited for NYC metro, ensemble max 2,000–3,000 J/kg; no direct Riverside sounding)
Buoy 44040 (WLIS_MET) observation 68 minutes old — over-water wind may have shifted since last reading
HRRR and NWS disagree on thunderstorm onset timing: HRRR keeps CAPE near zero through 1 PM, NWS forecast calls storms from 11 AM; smoke-driven CAPE uncertainty is the cause — NWS timing preferred
HRRR wind direction diverges 15° from western LIS station obs (model SSW 208°, obs SW 223°); using RYC 211° SSW as cove baseline
